# CARE v0.2.0-alpha.2

A maintenance follow-up to v0.2.0-alpha. This release ships the **first real OCR engine** (OnnxTR), fixes six concrete bugs surfaced by a post-merge audit, and reorganises the plugin code so future engines land in tens of lines instead of hundreds. Nothing in the public API or CLI surface has changed.

> **Pre-1.0.** Core invariants — offline-first, fail-closed, plugin-based, no original PDFs in public exports — remain stable. The API and CLI surface may still change before 1.0.

---

## Which installer should I download?

Pick the row that matches your network situation and whether you plan to use the optional ML plugins. **For most users on a connected workstation, `CARE-0.2.0-core-online-Setup.exe` is the right choice.**

| File | When to use |
|---|---|
| `CARE-0.2.0-core-online-Setup.exe` | **Default.** Slim install (~120 MB). Connected workstation. WebView2 runtime is fetched automatically if missing. |
| `CARE-0.2.0-core-airgap-Setup.exe` | Same as above but bundles the WebView2 runtime offline (~150 MB extra). Pick this if your machine cannot reach Microsoft's update servers. |
| `CARE-0.2.0-ml-online-Setup.exe` | Adds `torch` + `transformers` (~2 GB) so optional Hugging Face plugins can run. **Model weights are not bundled** — install them separately, see below. |
| `CARE-0.2.0-ml-airgap-Setup.exe` | Same ML libraries with the offline WebView2 runtime. |
| `CARE-0.2.0-core.msi` | Enterprise SCCM / Intune deployment, slim. WebView2 is a documented prerequisite admins pre-stage. |
| `CARE-0.2.0-ml.msi` | SCCM / Intune deployment with the ML libraries. |

Per-user install (no admin) is the default for the `.exe` SKUs. The installer dialog lets you elevate to per-machine if you want every account on the workstation to see the app. The `.msi` SKUs are per-machine only.

> **Asset names are unchanged from v0.2.0-alpha** — the file names embed the package version (`0.2.0`), not the tag suffix. If you have both alphas downloaded side-by-side, rename the older one or sort by date; the SHA-256 table at the bottom of each release page is the authoritative way to verify.

---

## What's new since v0.2.0-alpha

### First real OCR engine: **OnnxTR**

OnnxTR is the ONNX-runtime port of [docTR](https://github.com/mindee/doctr) — same detector / recognizer architectures, but inference runs through `onnxruntime` instead of PyTorch / TensorFlow. The trade-offs are deliberate:

- **No PyTorch dependency** in the install path. ~400 MB (`onnxruntime` + OpenCV + Pillow + scipy + numpy) instead of the ~2 GB `torch + transformers` adds. Stays out of the `ml` SKU entirely.
- **Local-files-only is a first-class code path.** Handing a local filesystem path to OnnxTR's model factory bypasses every network code path — no env-var hack, no monkeypatch.
- **Best in class for crash-report documents.** Independent benchmarks (e.g. [this one](https://ai.gopubby.com/i-tested-5-ocr-models-on-6-real-world-datasets-heres-which-one-you-should-actually-use-50badae3c16d) on FUNSD/IAM/SROIE) put OnnxTR / docTR first on printed forms (FUNSD score 0.589) and on degraded/noisy scans (0.398) — both directly relevant to scanned crash reports.

OnnxTR is **disabled by default** because its weights are operator-supplied; enable it in `config.yaml` after dropping the two `*.onnx` files into `models/ocr/onnxtr/`. See the new **Choosing an OCR engine** section in the README, plus [`docs/license-and-model-governance.md`](https://github.com/AhmedAredah/care/blob/main/docs/license-and-model-governance.md) for the activation checklist.

The Plugins page now shows OnnxTR with a **Tier B** accuracy badge carrying the FUNSD / IAM / SROIE numbers from the cited benchmark, plus a per-category breakdown so operators can see exactly where each engine is strong or weak before flipping it on.

### Mixed-PDF text-source routing — silent data loss fix

Earlier builds assumed every page in a PDF used the same text source: either the document had a native text layer or it didn't. Reports that combine the two — for example, a typed cover page followed by handwritten witness statements scanned and re-attached — went down a single code path, dropping content from whichever shape didn't match the document-level decision.

The pipeline now routes **per page**: pages with native text are extracted directly, pages without are rendered and OCR'd. The `FileInspection` payload carries `page_has_text` so the router can decide page-by-page, and the rasteriser accepts a `page_indices` filter so we don't re-render pages whose text we already have.

If you've previously processed mixed-source PDFs and noticed empty narrative blocks, those are the documents to re-run.

### Bug fixes from the post-OnnxTR audit

After OnnxTR landed, an audit of the surrounding code surfaced six concrete defects. All fixed in this release; each carries a regression test pinning the corrected behaviour.

- **`/api/plugins` now correctly reports `model_files_present` for ONNX, PaddleOCR, and Tesseract layouts.** The previous check insisted on `config.json` (a Hugging Face marker) for any directory configured, which made every non-HF provider look uninstalled in the GUI even when the right weights were present.
- **Native PDF text now defaults to `confidence = 1.0`.** The QA gate's `require_review_for_low_ocr_confidence` rule was silently bypassed on native pages because their confidence value was `None`. The gate now reasons uniformly across native and OCR'd pages.
- **VIN recognizer enforces the ISO 3779 / 49 CFR §565 mod-11 check digit.** Previously any 17-character string in the VIN alphabet matched, which flagged false positives on random IDs, transaction numbers, and OCR errors as VINs. Real VINs that fail the mod-11 check are caught; false positives that happen to fit the alphabet are rejected.
- **Date-of-birth recognizer rejects impossible calendar dates.** `02/30/1985`, `13/45/1990`, `01/02/3024`, `01/02/1875`, and any two-digit-year format are no longer matched. The recognizer parses through `datetime.date` to enforce a real calendar date in `[1900, today]`.
- **Windows CI now exercises the `tests/api` and `tests/integration` directories.** The pre-existing `tests/unit` job missed cross-platform issues in the Phase 6 API and integration suites — issues that surfaced post-merge instead of at PR time.
- **Version string is no longer hardcoded in `document_ir/builder.py`.** The builder now reads `care.__version__` (sourced from `importlib.metadata`) so it can never drift from `pyproject.toml` again.

### Plugin architecture cleanup

A chunk of provider boilerplate was duplicated across modules — file-format markers in the API layer, the same `find()` shape across thirteen PII recognizer files, the same offline-mode check at the top of every provider's `load()`, the entire HF token-classification lifecycle copy-pasted between Piiranha and RoBERTa-NER. Four refactors moved this knowledge to where it belongs:

- **`MODEL_DIR_KEYS` / `WEIGHT_MARKERS` now live on each provider class** (#22). The `/api/plugins` endpoint stopped enumerating supported config keys and weight extensions itself; it now asks each provider class. Adding a new OCR backend is no longer a multi-layer change.
- **PII recognizers share a `RegexRecognizer` base class** (#23). The thirteen recognizer modules collapsed to ~10 lines each, with `is_valid()` and `find()` overrides reserved for the recognizers that need them (VIN's check digit, DOB's calendar plausibility, signature's whitespace trim).
- **OCR providers share `assert_offline_config`** (#28). All three real OCR providers replaced 4–5 inline lines with one classmethod call, fixing a latent inconsistency along the way: Tesseract used to silently accept `local_files_only: false` while OnnxTR and PaddleOCR rejected it. Now everyone enforces the same contract.
- **HF token-classification providers share a mixin** (#29). Piiranha and RoBERTa-NER each shed ~140 lines of duplicated checksum walks, pipeline construction, and emit-loop scaffolding; only the policy differences (label-map vocabulary, drop-vs-fallback, manifest payload) stay on each concrete provider. Adding the next HF-shaped provider is now ~70 lines.

Net change: ~370 lines of provider boilerplate removed, no behavioural changes, every existing test still passes.

### Documentation

- **README has a new "Choosing an OCR engine" section** with a short benchmark-backed comparison table — explicit guidance on what to use for printed/scanned crash forms (OnnxTR), why PaddleOCR and EasyOCR are misfits despite being available in the registry, and when TrOCR-handwritten is worth the 2 GB.
- **`docs/license-and-model-governance.md` lists OnnxTR** in the provider table with its Apache-2.0 license posture and model-weight expectations.
- **PR descriptions for #22 / #23 / #28 / #29** stand as architecture notes for anyone wanting to read how the plugin classes are organised.

### Test portability

The `tests/api/test_shortcut.py` suite had three Windows-only-portable assumptions that surfaced when running on the new CI matrix; those assertions were rewritten to be platform-aware (#19).

---

## Installing optional ML model weights

The `ml` SKU bundles `torch` + `transformers`, but it does **not** include any model weights — those are licence-gated and operator-controlled by design. After installing the `ml` SKU:

1. Read [`docs/license-and-model-governance.md`](https://github.com/AhmedAredah/care/blob/main/docs/license-and-model-governance.md) for each model you intend to use.
2. Download the weights manually from the upstream source (Hugging Face, Paddle, etc.) on a machine that's allowed to.
3. Drop them under `%LOCALAPPDATA%\CARE\models\<category>\<provider-name>\` (e.g. `%LOCALAPPDATA%\CARE\models\pii\piiranha\` or `%LOCALAPPDATA%\CARE\models\ocr\onnxtr\`).
4. Enable the provider in the **Plugins** page; CARE refuses to load until model files are present and config-named.

OnnxTR weights ship as plain GitHub-Release artifacts of the [OnnxTR repo](https://github.com/felixdittrich92/OnnxTR/releases) — operators download out-of-band, drop into the configured `model_dir`, and either rename to the convention this provider expects or override the filename in config. No HF Hub roundtrip.

Piiranha and Kosmos-2.5 are flagged **license-review-required**; the GUI shows a warning chip until you've confirmed your organisation has authority to deploy them.

---

## SmartScreen / Windows Defender

These alpha binaries are **unsigned**. Code signing is provided free of charge to OSS by the [SignPath Foundation](https://signpath.io/foundation); CARE's enrolment is in progress. Until it completes, Windows SmartScreen will warn on first launch.

Two equivalent ways to clear the warning:

- **In Explorer:** right-click the downloaded `.exe` → **Properties** → tick **Unblock** → **OK**, then run normally.
- **In the SmartScreen dialog:** click **More info** → **Run anyway**.

Both options should only be used **after** verifying the SHA-256 against the table at the bottom of these notes.

---

## Verifying the download (recommended)

```powershell
# In PowerShell, in the directory where you saved the installer:
Get-FileHash .\CARE-0.2.0-core-online-Setup.exe -Algorithm SHA256
```

Compare the output with the SHA-256 listed at the bottom of this release. Any mismatch means the file is corrupted or tampered — re-download from this page.

---

## What this release is *not*

- **Not a crash-database scraper.** CARE runs on one workstation and writes redacted artifacts you then move into your own pipeline.
- **Not a privacy-by-magic tool.** The fail-closed gate exists because PII detection is imperfect; CARE refuses to ship a "good enough" redaction. Operators always own the final review.
- **Not a substitute for legal review.** Read the license-and-model-governance doc before enabling any optional model.
- **Not auto-on for OCR.** The default chain is `mock_ocr` so the demo runs without model files. Enable OnnxTR in `config.yaml` after dropping its weights into `models/ocr/onnxtr/`.

---

## Known limitations in this alpha

- OnnxTR is the recommended OCR engine but its **weights are operator-supplied** — the v0.2.x SKUs do not bundle them. Per-plugin SKUs (one core installer + opt-in `pii-ml`, `vlm`, `ocr-traditional`, `presidio`, `llm-local`, `llm-cloud` bundles) remain on the v0.3 roadmap.
- Optional ML providers (Piiranha, RoBERTa-NER, Kosmos-2.5, LayoutLM) ship the **loaders** in the `ml` SKU but require manual weight installation.
- Tier-A accuracy numbers from the regex PII provider are still from a **synthetic** corpus — a real held-out DOT-partner labelled set is targeted for v0.3.0.
- SignPath enrolment in progress — SmartScreen warning is expected.

---

## Upgrading from v0.2.0-alpha

In-place upgrade is supported — the installer detects the previous version and replaces it. No config migration is required: `config.yaml` from v0.2.0-alpha is forward-compatible. The new `onnxtr` block is already present in the shipped `config.yaml` (with `enabled: false`); reviewers who want to enable it should overlay just the `enabled: true` and `model_dir:` lines onto their existing config.

If you've previously processed mixed-source PDFs (some pages native-text, some scanned), consider re-running them — the silent-data-loss fix above means the new build extracts content the older builds dropped.

---

## Where to report issues

GitHub Issues: https://github.com/AhmedAredah/care/issues. Please include the contents of **Settings → Diagnostics** (it auto-scrubs paths and PII) when reporting.
